rules.rationale: DOCX tags lateral hip as classic GTPS while deep intra-articular conditions are groin/deep-groin, but pain location is unreliable and these conditions (especially the emergency Avascular Necrosis, whose risk-factor and night/rest-pain red flags would be hidden) can present atypically laterally, so down-weight rather than hard-exclude.

DOCX annotates buttock pain as non-hip origin (lumbar/SIJ/deep gluteal) and the JSON hip_q2 Buttock option carries a 'Consider lumbar spine / SIJ referral' note, so all hip conditions are down-weighted pending lumbar/SIJ assessment rather than excluded.
DOCX marks deep groin as classic for Labral Tear and AVN while GTPS is lateral and snapping is anterior/lateral, so GTPS and Snapping Hip are down-weighted (not excluded) for a deep-groin location.

DOCX attributes anterior snapping to iliopsoas (internal snapping hip) and locates GTPS at the lateral hip, so the lateral-tendon condition GTPS is down-weighted with an anterior snapping presentation.

DOCX flags radiation below the knee as lumbar origin, but it does not make a coexisting hip pathology impossible (a patient can have both lumbar radiculopathy and hip AVN/OA); hard-excluding would hide the AVN emergency red-flag questions, so down-weight all hip conditions instead of excluding.

DOCX names the snapping sensation the defining feature of Snapping Hip Syndrome and describes painless snapping as the benign form, making the other five (non-snapping) conditions less likely but not impossible.

DOCX ties sudden/twisting onset to Labral Tear and lists gradual/insidious onset as typical for OA, FAI, AVN and Snapping Hip, so those gradual-onset conditions are down-weighted (not excluded) with a sudden twisting onset.
